The **Canada men's national ice hockey team** (popularly known as **Team Canada**; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Équipe Canada*) is the [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team representing [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") internationally. The team is overseen by [Hockey Canada](/wiki/Hockey_Canada "Hockey Canada"), a member of the [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation"). From 1920 until 1963, Canada's international representation was by senior amateur club teams. Canada's national men's team was founded in 1963 by [David Bauer](/wiki/David_Bauer_(ice_hockey) "David Bauer (ice hockey)") as a part of the [Canadian Amateur Hockey Association](/wiki/Canadian_Amateur_Hockey_Association "Canadian Amateur Hockey Association"), playing out of the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia").[[4]](#cite_note-4)
The nickname "Team Canada" was first used for the 1972 [Summit Series](/wiki/Summit_Series "Summit Series") and has been frequently used to refer to both the Canadian national men's and women's teams ever since.

Canada is the most successful national ice hockey team in international play, having won the 1972 Summit Series against the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_ice_hockey_team "Soviet Union national ice hockey team"), a record four [Canada Cups](/wiki/Canada_Cup "Canada Cup") dating back to 1976, a record two [World Cups of Hockey](/wiki/World_Cup_of_Hockey "World Cup of Hockey"), a record nine [Olympic](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games") gold medals, and a record 28 [World Championship](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships") titles.

Canada is [one of the most successful national ice hockey teams in the world](/wiki/List_of_IIHF_World_Rankings "List of IIHF World Rankings") and a member of the so-called "[Big Six](/wiki/Big_Six_(ice_hockey) "Big Six (ice hockey)")", the unofficial group of the six strongest men's ice hockey nations, along with [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Sweden men's national ice hockey team"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team"), and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Hockey is Canada's national winter sport,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and Canadians are extremely passionate about the game. Canada was first represented internationally at the [1910 European Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_European_Championship_1910 "Ice Hockey European Championship 1910") by the [Oxford Canadians](/wiki/Oxford_Canadians "Oxford Canadians"), a team of Canadians from the University of Oxford. They represented Canada again at the [1912 World Championships](/wiki/1912_LIHG_Championship "1912 LIHG Championship").

From 1920 until 1963, the senior amateur club teams representing Canada, were usually the most recent [Allan Cup](/wiki/Allan_Cup "Allan Cup") champions. The last amateur club team from Canada to win a gold medal at the World Championship was the [Trail Smoke Eaters](/wiki/Trail_Smoke_Eaters_(senior) "Trail Smoke Eaters (senior)") in [1961](/wiki/1961_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1961 World Ice Hockey Championships"). The responsibility of choosing which team represented Canada belonged to [Canadian Amateur Hockey Association](/wiki/Canadian_Amateur_Hockey_Association "Canadian Amateur Hockey Association") (CAHA) secretary-manager; [George Dudley](/wiki/George_Dudley "George Dudley") from 1947 to 1960, and [Gordon Juckes](/wiki/Gordon_Juckes "Gordon Juckes") from 1960 to 1963.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Following the 1963 World Championships, [Father David Bauer](/wiki/David_Bauer_(ice_hockey) "David Bauer (ice hockey)") founded the national team as a permanent institution. The new permanent national team first competed in [ice hockey at the 1964 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1964 Winter Olympics"). His philosophy was to simply win the games against the weaker countries instead of running up the score.[[8]](#cite_note-Oliver-p120-8) Canada, Czechoslovakia and Sweden finished with identical records of five wins and two losses. Canada thought they had won the bronze medal based on the goal differential in the three games among the tied countries. When they attended the presentation of the Olympic medals, they were disappointed to learn they had finished in fourth place based on goal differential of all seven games played. The players and CAHA president [Art Potter](/wiki/Art_Potter "Art Potter") accused that [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF) president [Bunny Ahearne](/wiki/Bunny_Ahearne "Bunny Ahearne"), made a last-minute decision to change the rules and take away a medal from Canada.[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Marshall Johnston](/wiki/Marshall_Johnston "Marshall Johnston") summarized the team's feeling that, "The shepherd and his flock had been fleeced".[[8]](#cite_note-Oliver-p120-8)[[10]](#cite_note-O'Connor-10)

In 1967, the Canada men's national team was divided into western and eastern branches.[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Jack Bownass](/wiki/Jack_Bownass "Jack Bownass") coached the eastern team,[[12]](#cite_note-12) which played in the [Quebec Senior Hockey League](/wiki/Quebec_Senior_Hockey_League "Quebec Senior Hockey League") with [Derek Holmes](/wiki/Derek_Holmes_(ice_hockey) "Derek Holmes (ice hockey)") as its captain, to prepare for the [1968 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Before the Soviet Union began international competition in 1954, Canada dominated international hockey, winning six out of seven golds at the Olympics and 10 World Championship gold medals. Canada then went 50 years without winning the [Winter Olympic](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") gold medal, and from 1962 to 1993, did not win any World Championships. This was in part because Canada's best professional players were unable to attend these events as they had commitments with their [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") teams.

Canada was awarded hosting duties of the [1970 Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/1970_Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "1970 Ice Hockey World Championships") with the limited use of former professionals. The IIHF later reversed the permission after [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") president [Avery Brundage](/wiki/Avery_Brundage "Avery Brundage") objected to professionals at an amateur event. CAHA president [Earl Dawson](/wiki/Earl_Dawson "Earl Dawson") withdrew the national team from international competitions against European hockey teams until Canada was allowed to use its best players.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

While boycotting the IIHF, other international competitions were held such as the 1972 Canada–USSR [Summit Series](/wiki/Summit_Series "Summit Series") and in 1976 the inaugural [Canada Cup](/wiki/Canada_Cup "Canada Cup") invitational. Canada returned to the IIHF in 1977 after a series of negotiations between IIHF President [Günther Sabetzki](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Sabetzki "Günther Sabetzki") and top Canadian and American professional ice hockey officials. As a result, professionals are allowed to compete at the World Championship which was scheduled later in the year to ensure more players are available from among the [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") teams eliminated from the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") playoffs. In return, a competition for the Canada Cup was to be played every four years on North American territory with the participation of Canada, the United States, and the four strongest European national teams, including professionals.

In 1983, Hockey Canada began the "Program of Excellence", whose purpose was to prepare a team for the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the Winter Olympics") every four years. This new National Team played a full season together all over the world against both national and club teams, and often attracted top NHL prospects. In 1986, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") voted to allow professional athletes to compete in Olympic Games, starting in 1988.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Veteran pros with NHL experience and, in a few cases, current NHLers who were holding out in contract disputes joined the team. This program was discontinued in 1998, when the NHL began shutting down to allow its players to compete.

After not winning a gold medal for 33 years, Canada won the [1994 World Championship](/wiki/1994_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1994 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships") in Italy. Since that time, they have won in 1997, 2003, 2004, 2007, 2015, 2016, 2021 and 2023. Canada captured its first Olympic gold medal in 50 years at [Salt Lake City 2002](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics"). At [Vancouver 2010](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2010 Winter Olympics"), Canada won the gold medal with a 3–2 win against the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team") in the final. [Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby")'s overtime goal secured Canada the final gold medal awarded at the Games.[[16]](#cite_note-16) At the [2012 World Championship](/wiki/2012_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2012 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships") in Finland and Sweden, [Ryan Murray](/wiki/Ryan_Murray "Ryan Murray") became the first draft eligible prospect to represent Canada at the [Ice Hockey World Championship](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championship "Ice Hockey World Championship").

Canada successfully defended gold at [Sochi 2014](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2014 Winter Olympics"), becoming the first men's team to do so since the Soviet Union in [1988](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1988 Winter Olympics"), the first to finish the tournament undefeated since [1984](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1984_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1984 Winter Olympics") and the first to do both with a full NHL participation. Their relentless offensive pressure and stifling defence has earned the [2014 squad](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_team_rosters#Canada "Ice hockey at the 2014 Winter Olympics – Men's team rosters") praise as perhaps the best, most complete Team Canada ever assembled.[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Drew Doughty](/wiki/Drew_Doughty "Drew Doughty") and [Shea Weber](/wiki/Shea_Weber "Shea Weber") led the team in scoring, while [Jonathan Toews](/wiki/Jonathan_Toews "Jonathan Toews") scored the gold medal-winning goal in the first period of a 3–0 win over Sweden in the final. The architect behind the 2010 and 2014 teams, [Steve Yzerman](/wiki/Steve_Yzerman "Steve Yzerman"), immediately stepped down as general manager following the win.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Led by general manager [Jim Nill](/wiki/Jim_Nill "Jim Nill"), head coach [Todd McLellan](/wiki/Todd_McLellan "Todd McLellan"), and the late addition of captain [Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby"), Canada won the [2015 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2015_IIHF_World_Championship "2015 IIHF World Championship") in dominating fashion over [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team"), their first win at the Worlds since [2007](/wiki/2007_IIHF_World_Championship "2007 IIHF World Championship"). By winning all 10 of their games in regulation, [Hockey Canada](/wiki/Hockey_Canada "Hockey Canada") was awarded a 1 million Swiss franc bonus prize in the first year of its existence.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Canada scored 66 goals in their 10 games and had the top three scorers of the tournament: [Jason Spezza](/wiki/Jason_Spezza "Jason Spezza"), [Jordan Eberle](/wiki/Jordan_Eberle "Jordan Eberle") and [Taylor Hall](/wiki/Taylor_Hall "Taylor Hall"). [Tyler Seguin](/wiki/Tyler_Seguin "Tyler Seguin") also led the championship with nine goals. The win secured Canada's return to number one on the IIHF world rankings for the first time since 2010.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

At the [2021 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2021_IIHF_World_Championship "2021 IIHF World Championship"), following a cancelled [2020 tournament](/wiki/2020_IIHF_World_Championship "2020 IIHF World Championship") due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), Canada returned to the competition with a roster weaker than most years, featuring rare inclusions of draft prospects and other non-NHL prospects. The team lost three games in regulation to start the tournament, the first Canadian team in Worlds history to do so,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and needed 10 points over the final four round robin games to make the playoff round. Winning the tiebreaker over Kazakhstan, Canada qualified for the playoff round as the lowest seed and managed wins over Russia and the United States before playing Finland for a rematch of the [2019 final](/wiki/2019_IIHF_World_Championship_final "2019 IIHF World Championship final") in the [gold medal game](/wiki/2021_IIHF_World_Championship_final "2021 IIHF World Championship final"). [Nick Paul](/wiki/Nick_Paul "Nick Paul")'s goal won the game for Canada in overtime, despite the Finns having either led or been tied the entire game, capping off a most unlikely Canadian IIHF men's gold.

### Summit Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Summit Series")]

* [1972](/wiki/Summit_Series "Summit Series") – **Winners**
* [1974](/wiki/1974_Summit_Series "1974 Summit Series") – *Runners-up*

On the 40th anniversary of the 1972 Summit Series, the [IIHF Milestone Award](/wiki/IIHF_Milestone_Award "IIHF Milestone Award") was given to the Canadian and Russian teams for the event which had a "decisive influence on the development of the game".[[44]](#cite_note-Reuters-2012-45) [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") wrote that Canada was expected to win the series easily, but when they came from behind to win in the eighth and final game, it marked "the beginning of the modern hockey era".[[44]](#cite_note-Reuters-2012-45)

### Spengler Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Spengler Cup")]

In the [Spengler Cup](/wiki/Spengler_Cup "Spengler Cup"), Team Canada competes against European club teams, such as [HC Davos](/wiki/HC_Davos "HC Davos") who host the tournament every year in [Eisstadion Davos](/wiki/Eisstadion_Davos "Eisstadion Davos"). Canada used to be represented by the standing national team at this event, but is now usually made up of Canadians playing in European leagues or the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"). In [2019](/wiki/2019_Spengler_Cup "2019 Spengler Cup"), Team Canada won its 16th Spengler Cup, passing the host team HC Davos for the most titles. HC Davos is now tied for most wins after winning in [2023](/wiki/2023_Spengler_Cup "2023 Spengler Cup").

### 2026 Winter Olympics roster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2026 Winter Olympics roster")]

The first six players of Canada's roster were announced on June 16, 2025.[[45]](#cite_note-49) The remainder of the team was named on December 31, 2025.[[46]](#cite_note-50)[[47]](#cite_note-51)[[48]](#cite_note-52) On February 3, 2026, [Anthony Cirelli](/wiki/Anthony_Cirelli "Anthony Cirelli") was ruled out due to injury, with [Sam Bennett](/wiki/Sam_Bennett_(ice_hockey) "Sam Bennett (ice hockey)") named as his replacement.[[49]](#cite_note-53) On February 5, [Brayden Point](/wiki/Brayden_Point "Brayden Point") was also ruled out and replaced by [Seth Jarvis](/wiki/Seth_Jarvis "Seth Jarvis").[[50]](#cite_note-54) On February 8, 2026, Canada named [Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby") as team [captain](/wiki/Captain_(ice_hockey) "Captain (ice hockey)"), with [Connor McDavid](/wiki/Connor_McDavid "Connor McDavid") and [Cale Makar](/wiki/Cale_Makar "Cale Makar") serving as alternate captains.[[51]](#cite_note-55) Following an injury to Crosby in Canada's quarterfinal game against Czechia, McDavid served as captain for their semifinal game against Finland and the gold medal game against the United States, with [Nathan MacKinnon](/wiki/Nathan_MacKinnon "Nathan MacKinnon") serving as an additional alternate captain for both games.[[52]](#cite_note-56)[[53]](#cite_note-57)
